post-site-visit-blacktown.title: Site Visit 2: A Visit to Western Sydney - Blacktown and the Western Sydney Parklands
post-site-visit-blacktown.lead: Blacktown City Council & Greater Sydney Parklands
post-site-visit-blacktown.summary:
Site visit to Blacktown, led by Blacktown City Council
Location: Fairwater Estate (Blacktown) & Bungarribee Park (Western Sydney Parklands)
post-site-visit-blacktown.notes:
Journey to Western Sydney for a full day exploring how communities on the city’s fast-growing region are balancing rapid urban development with accessible, health-supportive green space. 

The morning begins in Blacktown with a visit to the Fairwater Estate, offering first-hand insight into contemporary residential growth in one of Sydney’s most diverse and fastest-changing local government areas. Participants will see how new housing, infrastructure and community amenity are being shaped, and consider the challenges and opportunities of delivering liveable, healthy neighbourhoods at scale. 

At midday, the group will gather for an authentic Aussie BBQ lunch in the park, a relaxed opportunity to share ideas, network and enjoy the outdoors the way locals do. 

The afternoon continues at Bungarribee in the Western Sydney Parklands. At over 27 square kilometres, the Western Sydney Parklands is one of the largest urban parklands in the world. Bungarribee Park, the largest recreational space within the Western Sydney Parklands, is a flagship example of a vibrant, health-supportive community destination for the rapidly growing populations of Western Sydney. 

Guided by Greater Sydney Parklands, participants will explore how the park supports recreation, biodiversity, social connection and everyday physical activity. Highlights include the extensive network of shared walking and cycling trails winding through restored bushland and open parkland; the award-winning nature-based adventure playground with its climbing tower, flying foxes and water and sand play areas; expansive picnic and BBQ shelters; and dedicated off-leash dog areas. Along the way, the group will consider how the parklands are planned, activated and maintained to serve one of Australia’s most diverse and fastest-growing regions. 

The visit offers valuable lessons for practitioners, policymakers and researchers on embedding large-scale, accessible and biodiverse green space within expanding metropolitan areas — and on the ongoing work of activating these spaces so communities genuinely use and benefit from them.

pre-site-visit-centennial.venue: WILD PLAY Discovery Centre in Centennial Park
pre-site-visit-centennial.summary:
WILD PLAY Discovery Centre in Centennial Park (Google map: https://maps.app.goo.gl/9DJDkyqAzABxxpU67)  
Capacity: 80 pax
pre-site-visit-centennial.notes:
Urban green spaces are widely recognised for their role in supporting health and wellbeing, yet many cities continue to focus on infrastructure rather than how these spaces are actively used. This session responds to that gap by demonstrating a simple, low-resource approach to activating green spaces through facilitated nature connection practices. It introduces an entry-level model that can be delivered on-site and adapted to digital or hybrid formats.  

Grounded in practitioner experience from Greater Sydney Parklands and supported by research across early childhood, adolescent and adult programs, this session explores why nature connection matters and how it can be created with minimal resources. Evidence from programs such as Guardians of the Park, Bush School and The Ian Potter Children’s WILD PLAY Garden shows that repeated, facilitated engagement with nature supports wellbeing, social connection and pro-environmental behaviour across the life course.  

This hands-on workshop will be of interest to practitioners, policymakers and community organisations seeking practical and transferable approaches. Participants will leave with a clear understanding of how facilitation — rather than infrastructure — activates green spaces, and with adaptable tools that can be applied immediately in diverse urban contexts.

pre-site-visit-greenway.lead: Professor Susan Thompson (School of Built Environment, UNSW)
pre-site-visit-greenway.notes:
Situated in a rapidly growing and densifying mixed residential area, the GreenWay is a 6.7 km multifunctional green corridor. It incorporates major stormwater and light rail infrastructure, a shared walking / cycling path, bush care sites, recreational facilities, heritage and Indigenous acknowledgement, and public art installations. The GreenWay started back in 1988 as a grassroots campaign led by local residents, environmental advocates and cycling groups. Over time, it secured multi-level government support and was delivered incrementally across political cycles. The final sections opened in late 2025, completing an $AU60 million multi-government project. 

The walk will commence at Iron Cove and go the full length of the GreenWay to the Cooks River. Along the way we will see different features of the GreenWay and experience first-hand its evolution from local community advocacy to an important recreational, educational and commuter corridor offering health supportive activities for local residents and visitors. During the walk there will be opportunities to consider a range of issues which offer lessons for the development of greenways in other cities. This will include challenges of ongoing maintenance, community participation in environmental programs and opportunities for education. 

What to bring and wear: Hat, sunscreen, rain jacket and water bottle; wear closed in comfortable walking shoes 

Background reading: ‘Learning Along the GreenWay: An Experiential, Transdisciplinary 

Outdoor Classroom for Planetary Health Education’. This paper situates the GreenWay within a broader global movement of reclaiming urban corridors for nature, movement, health supportive community life and education.  

Inner West Council GreenWay Website. Here you will find further information on the Greenway’s shared walking and cycling infrastructure, parks and public art, bushcare and biodiversity initiatives.
